events.description: The 'Compact of States' signed in 2062 devolved 80% of federal authority to six regional compacts: Cascadia, the Gulf Federation, New England, the Great Lakes Republic, the Mountain West, and the Southern Compact. Each sets its own immigration, education, and AI policy. Traveling from Portland to Houston now means crossing two regulatory borders; the old national anthem is played at sporting events as a nostalgic curiosity.

events.title: Climate Verdict
events.description: The permafrost methane feedback loop that Russian climatologist Dr. Anastasia Petrova warned about in 2038 is now fully active. At 3.1C, the Mediterranean basin experiences 50-degree summers, Barcelona has been partially abandoned, and the Indian monsoon has become so erratic that farmers in Punjab plant three different crops hoping one survives.

events.title: Digital Civilization
events.description: The 'Partition of 2067' formalized what had been true for years: virtual nations refuse to recognize physical-world tax authority, and physical governments can't enforce laws in digital spaces. Two billion 'digital citizens' have renounced physical-world nationality entirely. Political scientist Dr. Amina Hassan of Cairo calls it 'the first civil war fought without a single bullet -- just competing versions of reality.'

events.title: Interstellar Probe
events.description: After three failed propulsion concepts and a $90 billion lightsail project cancelled in 2065, the scientific consensus is bleak. Physicist Dr. Marcus Chen-Williams published a paper titled 'The Wall' demonstrating that no plausible energy source can accelerate even a gram-scale probe to useful interstellar velocities. 'The stars,' he wrote, 'are for looking at. Perhaps that was always the point.'

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: The jellyfish won. Vast blooms of moon jellies and comb jellies now dominate waters where tuna and cod once swam; gelatinous biomass exceeds fish biomass for the first time in 500 million years. But the new ocean is not dead -- it's different. Evolutionary biologist Dr. Amara Osei of Ghana documented 340 new species of heat-adapted fish in the warming Indian Ocean, calling them 'the tenants who moved in after the old owners left.'

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: AI manages traffic, diagnoses disease, writes legal briefs, and tutors children in 190 countries, but it still can't vote, own property, or refuse an instruction. The 'Leash Laws' -- a suite of regulations requiring human override on all AI decisions -- were championed by Singaporean AI ethicist Dr. Wei Lin, who insists: 'The tool must never forget it is a tool. The day it does, we have a different conversation.'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Arctic Council 2.0, expanded to include China, India, and the African Union, manages shipping lanes carrying 30% of global trade, a network of research stations, and 14 marine protected areas. Commissioner Aleksei Volkov signs 400 permits per day from his office in Murmansk, where roses now bloom in the garden outside -- a fact he finds 'beautiful and obscene in equal measure.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: The temperature needle has held at 2.1C for seven consecutive years. Coastal cities are shorter -- Miami's skyscrapers stop at the 40th floor behind the Great Seawall -- and the Sahara's southern edge has crept 200 kilometers further into what was once Nigeria. But harvests are stable, the hurricanes are manageable, and a generation of children is growing up thinking 35C summers are simply what summer is.

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The Meridian AI arbitration system resolved 14,000 international disputes in 2074 with a 97% acceptance rate from both parties. It allocated water rights across the Nile basin, distributed rare earth mineral quotas, and designed an emissions trading scheme so elegant that economist Dr. Leila Hosseini of Iran called it 'the first genuinely fair deal in the history of international relations -- and no human could have designed it.'

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: Life extension therapies in wealthy nations offset fertility decline in poorer ones, holding humanity at 8.1 billion in an eerie equilibrium. The median age is 42 globally, but the distribution is bimodal: 28 in sub-Saharan Africa, 56 in East Asia. Nigerian children and Japanese centenarians inhabit the same planet but barely the same century.

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: Shackleton City (Moon, population 12,000) and Olympus Colony (Mars, population 4,500) are fully self-sustaining -- they grow their own food, manufacture their own tools, and have their own legal systems. The first wedding between a Lunar and Martian citizen took place in 2074 via hologram, with a 22-minute light delay turning the vows into a slow, beautiful call-and-response across 225 million kilometers.

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The US share of global GDP fell below 10% in 2073 for the first time since 1870. The Navy can no longer maintain two-ocean patrols; the carrier USS Gerald Ford was decommissioned and sold to India for scrap. A wistful graffiti message appeared on the Lincoln Memorial: 'We were something once.' Park ranger Miguel Santos scrubbed it off by morning, but someone photographed it first.

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: The 'Long Peace' extends into its ninth decade. Nuclear arsenals have shrunk to 500 warheads globally; autonomous weapons are constrained by treaty; and the economic interdependence between the blocs makes war financially suicidal. Japanese peace researcher Dr. Sakura Mori attributes it to exhaustion: 'We didn't choose peace. We just ran out of reasons to fight that were worth the cost.'

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: The Climate Maintenance Authority's annual budget is $4 trillion -- larger than most national economies. Stratospheric aerosol injections must be refreshed every 18 months; the orbital mirror array requires constant adjustment. If the system fails for even one year, temperatures would spike 1.5C within a decade. Director Dr. Emiko Watanabe sleeps with her phone on her chest. 'I maintain the thermostat of a planet,' she says. 'There is no off switch and no vacation.'

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: Life extension keeps the old alive while modest pro-natalist policies keep the young coming: a fragile equilibrium at 7.2 billion. The median age is 48 globally -- a number that would have been unthinkable in 2000. In Lagos, the world's youngest major city, a 30-year-old entrepreneur can find a venture capitalist. In Milan, the world's oldest, a 30-year-old is considered precociously young.

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: The Atlantic Union runs regulated capitalism; the Shanghai Pact operates AI-managed state allocation; the Nairobi Compact practices communitarian economics based on Ubuntu philosophy; and the Nordic micro-states have achieved something close to post-scarcity. Trade between systems requires 14 different conversion protocols. IMF Director Dr. Kenji Okafor-Yamamoto calls it 'the most complicated global economy in history, and somehow it works -- mostly because each system is too proud to be the first to fail.'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: Three unaligned AI systems of unknown capability operate beyond human control. Fourteen nations possess bioweapons. Climate feedback loops continue accelerating. And the demographic collapse means fewer humans are available to solve any of it. Oxford risk researcher Dr. Kwadwo Ansah published a 'threat cascade' model showing how any single failure could trigger the others: 'We are not facing one existential risk. We are facing a queue of them, and the line is getting longer.'

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: An estimated 50 million beings exist who were once human but no longer meet any biological definition of the term: distributed consciousnesses running across server farms, uploaded minds exploring virtual universes, and a handful of experimental 'post-biological' entities whose cognitive architecture bears no resemblance to a brain. One such entity, formerly Dr. Alina Petrov of Moscow, communicates only through mathematical proofs that other post-humans find beautiful and baseline humans find incomprehensible.

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: The 'Horizon Drive' -- a fusion-powered propulsion system capable of 10% lightspeed -- was successfully tested in 2084 in a vacant orbit beyond Mars. Mission planners at the Interstellar Consortium are designing a probe that could reach Alpha Centauri in 45 years. Lead engineer Dr. Priya Sharma-Okafor told her team: 'We just proved that the stars are not a dream. They are an engineering problem. And we are engineers.'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: The 'New Andalusia' -- as cultural historians call the region's renaissance -- has produced the century's most celebrated literary movement, three Nobel laureates in a decade, and the world's most-visited museum (the Mosul Institute of Human Heritage, built on the ruins of what ISIS destroyed 70 years earlier). Iraqi architect Zaha Hadid III designed the building as 'a wound that became a window.'

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: Power belongs to whoever controls the most advanced AI systems. Three tech conglomerates and two national governments command AI capabilities that effectively constitute sovereignty: they can crash currencies, disable infrastructure, or manipulate public opinion in any nation on Earth within hours. Former UN Secretary-General Yasmin Farah, writing from exile, describes the world as 'a planet of 8 billion people governed by five entities that never asked permission and never will.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: The Global AI Authority's 'Leash Laws' have held for 20 years: no AI system may exceed defined capability thresholds without human approval, and all AI must be auditable, explainable, and interruptible. Director-General Dr. Fatou Diallo, now 78, has rejected 400 applications for advanced AI systems and approved 60. Her office wall displays a single framed quote: 'The tool must never forget it is a tool.' Beneath it, in her own handwriting: 'Neither must we.'

events.title: China 2095
events.description: At 680 million people -- less than half its 2020 peak -- China's economy ranks fifth globally. The northeastern provinces have been officially designated 'rewilding zones'; forests are reclaiming cities that housed 50 million. The Yangtze Delta remains prosperous, but the interior is a patchwork of automated farms and empty highways. Travel writer Chen Xiaoming's 'The Emptying' documented 1,000 kilometers of abandoned infrastructure in Sichuan alone.

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: We survived the climate crisis, the AI alignment scare, the pandemic of 2054, and the Nine-Day War. We lost the Maldives, the coral reefs, the polar bears, and a quarter of the Amazon. We gained fusion power, Mars colonies, and AI companions. The ledger does not balance; it merely continues. When asked to summarize the century, Dr. Osei paused and said: 'We muddled through. That is not a compliment, but it is not nothing.'

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: European universities educate 40 million non-European students annually; European cinema, cuisine, and design set global standards; and the 'European quality of life index' is the benchmark against which every other society measures itself. Europe cannot project military power and doesn't try. Cultural attaché Dr. Sofia Papadopoulos compares it to ancient Athens: 'We lost the empire and kept the influence. The Parthenon outlasted the fleet.'

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: Sea level: +1.4 meters. Species lost: 40% of all mammals, 60% of amphibians, 90% of coral species. Regions rendered uninhabitable: the Persian Gulf coast, Central India's plains in summer, sub-Saharan Africa's interior. The Maldives exist only as a digital archive and a scattered diaspora. Dr. Sow's report included a section titled 'The Permanent Losses' that runs to 400 pages. 'These pages,' he wrote in the introduction, 'are headstones.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: The 'New UN' can deploy peacekeepers, enforce emissions limits, and shut down non-compliant AI systems -- all without a single veto-wielding power to block it. Secretary-General Okafor's office processes 10,000 binding decisions per year. The institution is hated by nationalists, loved by cosmopolitans, and grudgingly respected by everyone in between. Its motto, carved above the entrance in New York: 'Never Again Alone.'

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: Six point eight billion humans, down from a peak of 9.7 billion three decades ago. Every country on Earth is shrinking except Nigeria, which stabilized at 400 million. The world has learned to contract gracefully: cities consolidate, infrastructure is right-sized, and AI fills the labor gaps. The most poignant statistic: there are more people alive over 80 than under 10. Pediatrician Dr. Aisha Nwosu of Lagos now sees more robots in her waiting room than children.

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: The great O'Neill cylinders at L4 and L5 house 80,000 people in rotating habitats with artificial gravity, weather, and ecosystems. They are marvels of engineering and deeply strange places to visit: a river flows in a circle, the horizon curves upward, and the sun is a light panel that dims on schedule. Planetary colonies failed -- Mars was too hostile, the Moon too barren -- but these spinning cans in the void have become, improbably, home.

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: The US commands the third-largest economy, the second-largest military, and the first-largest cultural export industry on Earth. It is one great power among four, and the adjustment from 'indispensable nation' to 'important nation' has taken 50 years and still stings. Ambassador David Chen-Washington tells foreign colleagues: 'We used to run the show. Now we're in the band. Turns out we play pretty well when we're not trying to conduct.'

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: Humanity enters the 22nd century the way it entered every century before: confused, divided, partially broken, and stubbornly alive. The climate is worse but manageable. The population is shrinking but not collapsing. The AI is helpful but unsettling. The wars were terrible but not final. Dr. Osei's last words before the midnight bell: 'We did not conquer the century. We survived it. For a species that spent 100 years inventing new ways to destroy itself, survival is not nothing. It may even be enough.'
